questions.question: The diagram shows a box of weight $50\text{ N}$ with dimensions $0.1\text{ m} \times 0.2\text{ m} \times 0.4\text{ m}$. What is the maximum pressure the box can exert on a table?
options: $2500\text{ Pa}$
$625\text{ Pa}$
$1250\text{ Pa}$
$500\text{ Pa}$
questions.explanation:
Smallest area $= 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02\text{ m}^2$. Max pressure $= 50 / 0.02 = 2500\text{ Pa}$. 

**Common mistake:** Calculating pressure using the largest face ($0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08 rightarrow 625\text{ Pa}$).

questions.question: Which set of conditions will produce the highest pressure on a surface?
options: Large force acting on a small area
Large force acting on a large area
Small force acting on a small area
Small force acting on a large area
questions.explanation:
Pressure is directly proportional to force and inversely proportional to area. Therefore, a large force on a small area creates the maximum pressure. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a large area is required for high pressure.

questions.question: A table has a mass of $20\text{ kg}$. It has four identical legs, each with a contact area of $0.001\text{ m}^2$. What is the pressure exerted by each leg on the floor? (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
options: $5000\text{ Pa}$
questions.explanation:
Total weight $= 200\text{ N}$. Weight per leg $= 200 / 4 = 50\text{ N}$. Pressure per leg $= 50 / 0.001 = 50000\text{ Pa}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the total weight ($200$) for a single leg ($200000$).

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies the relationship between force, area, and pressure?
options: Force: Increase; Area: Same; Pressure: Increase
Force: Decrease; Area: Same; Pressure: Increase
Force: Same; Area: Increase; Pressure: Increase
Force: Increase; Area: Increase; Pressure: Same
questions.explanation:
According to the 'Let's Map It' section, for the same area, the greater the force, the greater the pressure. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking increasing area increases pressure.

questions.question: A graph is plotted with Pressure ($y$-axis) against Area ($x$-axis) for a constant applied force. Which shape will the graph have?
options: A curve that decreases as area increases
A straight line through the origin
A horizontal straight line
A vertical straight line
questions.explanation:
Since $p = F / A$, $p  and A$ are inversely proportional. This results in a hyperbola (decreasing curve). 

**Common mistake:** Thinking it is a straight line, which only happens if pressure is plotted against $1/Area.
